Hi Robert, i have a question regarding your video of the microwave transformer, and i write it down here since this video is brand new and you're more likely to see my comment.
To make the bolt melt faster, you added more turns in your secondary wire, but doesn't it lower the amps going through the bolt ?

author: @soultracer
text:
I try to make a constructive critic about the Sound of the Videos, because it is getting worse. 
It sounds like, you are using your camera-microphone in a very reverberate room.
So I see, you are already using a device with a lavaliere microphone, but I don't think this is working good.
If you take a good audio recording device with a good lavaliere microphone, the sound would be better,
because these can remove reverberation and noise. 
In this video, the sound is also not synchronized. 
I love your videos, it's interesting to listen to you, and bad sound makes me sad. ;-)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i re listened to the sound and i just don't hear it - it sounds ok to me - sometimes a bit of echo sure - but it sounds fine to me

author: @TimeSurfer206

The heat exchanger was a new one for me, and a brilliant thought.

author: @higreentj
text: Building with hempcrete would give us highly insulated mould free homes and help end global warming by locking up carbon for hundreds of years. I wonder if gyroids could be used to strengthen hempcrete walls?

author: @miriam-english
text: Thanks Robert. I hadn't heard of gyroids before, but looking at it, it immediately struck me how good they could be for supercapacitors. The two "channels" could be the plates of the capacitor and the gyroid the insulator separating them. They smaller these were made, the higher the surface area and the greater the storage.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I think you are right - supercaps are all about surface area

author: @atrumluminarium
text: Small correction: that shape with the soap film is actually a catenoid, a gyroid is different. Granted they are both minimal surfaces but they have no similarities apart from the mean curvature being everywhere 0, they are even in a different so called associate family (basically surfaces within the same family can be transformed from one to another continuously and isometrically).

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate thanks for the correction

replies.author: @atrumluminarium
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering an interesting application that's easily DIY I think would be for a supercapacitor. Since the capacitance is a function of the overlapping area, I can picture a print of the 2 halves being made out of conductive (graphene?) filament and then using epoxy as the dielectric 😁

replies.author: @dSquared0162
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Thanks but I don't know about that yet... Full time job and so many projects. Currently installing a heat pump, have a half printed 'aeromine' derivative, a half-printed folding 'harmony', a half-printed 'archimedes', a printed but not deployed 'darwin', I've got the bits and pieces for a switched atmospheric generator but not put them together yet, and various versions of pulse-charging battery charger prototypes have come and gone, and a host of other started-but-waiting-for-life-to-stop-interrupting-what-I-want-to-do projects... If I manage to get just one of them completed without electrocuting myself first I'll be happy to pass on the details! 🤣I did manage to install some (functional!) solar panels. Most of these projects, of course, started after one of your inspiring videos. Thank you!   👍

author: @grunwode
text: I find their ability to move separately from each other intriguing, especially as it requires no loss casting process. Mayhaps there is also some application for stationary phase engineering in separations columns.
